does: This paper investigates an empirical inconsistency between theoretical expectations and practical Transformer behavior regarding the residual stream. While mathematical theory predicts that individual coordinates in the residual stream should be interchangeable and carry no special significance, the authors demonstrate that certain dimensions consistently exhibit privileged status across different models and tasks. Through careful experimentation and analysis, they trace this phenomenon to the per-dimension adaptive learning rates in the Adam optimizer, which create unequal treatment of different coordinate dimensions during training. The work provides empirical evidence for this mechanism and explores its implications for how Transformers actually learn and represent information.
connects: This research builds on recent interpretability work examining the internal structure and function of Transformer residual streams, extending prior observations that practical networks deviate from theoretical predictions about coordinate independence. It directly responds to theoretical analyses suggesting residual stream coordinates should be symmetric, drawing connections to broader work in mechanistic interpretability that reveals gaps between formal theory and empirical reality in deep learning. The findings also relate to ongoing investigations into optimizer effects on learned representations and the role of normalization in shaping neural network behavior. The paper engages with foundational understanding of how Transformer components interact and how optimization dynamics shape the learned solution.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more principled understanding of optimizer effects on Transformer learning dynamics and representation formation. It suggests potential improvements to training procedures that account for coordinate privileging, either through modified optimizers that explicitly handle symmetry or through better initialization schemes. The findings could inform better theoretical models of Transformers that incorporate realistic optimizer behavior rather than idealized assumptions. Future work might explore whether privileged bases emerge systematically across architectures, whether they correlate with model capabilities or interpretability, and whether controlling these effects during training improves performance or alignment properties. The research points toward tighter integration between optimization theory and Transformer architecture analysis.
